key_insight: In this open-label pilot, a single 25 mg psilocybin dose delivered with multidisciplinary palliative care support appeared safe, feasible, and highly acceptable for cancer patients with demoralization and chronic pain.
clinical_relevance: The study suggests psilocybin-assisted therapy may become a supportive palliative option for addressing both existential distress and pain in oncology care, if confirmed in larger controlled trials.
method: Single 25 mg oral psilocybin dose with preparation, monitoring, and integration
Support from mental health, spiritual health, and multidisciplinary palliative care clinicians
evidence_signal: Among the 10 dosed participants, there were no serious adverse events, complete visit retention, and most reported meaningful and spiritually significant experiences with improved demoralization and pain scores by study end.
watch_item: This was a small open-label pilot without a control group, so results are preliminary and may not generalize to broader cancer populations or routine clinical implementation.
paper_title: Psilocybin-Assisted Early Palliative Care for Demoralization and Chronic Pain: An Open-Label Pilot Study
